participants.extracted: Forty-six sedentary obese Puerto Rican adults (men and women) aged 22–50 years were screened, and 30 were enrolled after excluding those with calcium intake above 700 mg/day or who withdrew before baseline. Participants had BMI ≥30 kg/m², no chronic conditions, were not taking calcium supplements or regular medications, and were not lactose intolerant; women could not be pregnant or lactating. Of the 30 randomized subjects, 25 completed the 21-week study (20 women and 5 men), with the completed sample distributed across the high dairy, high calcium, and control groups.

interventions.extracted: Participants were randomized for 21 weeks to one of three arms: a high-dairy diet, a high-calcium supplement diet, or control. The high-dairy group was instructed to consume four daily servings of low-fat dairy products (milk, cheese, and yogurt) to achieve a total calcium intake of about 1200–1300 mg/day, while substituting other foods to keep the diet isocaloric. The high-calcium group continued their usual diet plus a 600 mg/day calcium carbonate supplement, targeting a total calcium intake of about 1200–1300 mg/day. The control group continued their usual diet plus a placebo tablet, with total calcium intake intended to remain below 700 mg/day. All groups were asked to maintain their usual physical activity during the study.

outcomes.extracted: The trial planned to measure body composition and serum lipid outcomes over 21 weeks in obese adults assigned to high dairy, high calcium supplement, or control conditions. Body weight (kg) was measured monthly throughout the 21-week study. Body mass index (kg/m²) was calculated from measured weight and height; height was measured at baseline and BMI was assessed at baseline and 21 weeks. Dual-energy X-ray absorptiometry (DEXA) was used at baseline and 21 weeks to assess total body lean mass (kg), total body fat mass (kg), total body fat percentage (%), trunk body fat mass (kg), trunk body fat percentage (%), total bone mineral content (TBMC), and total bone mineral density (TBMD). Fasting serum lipids were measured at baseline and 21 weeks, including total cholesterol, LDL cholesterol, HDL cholesterol, and triacylglycerol levels (reported from colorimetric assay; LDL and triacylglycerol were log-transformed for analysis).
